ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Resizable Circular Queue with Fixed Array and Manual Resize
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a dynamic circular buffer using array resizing strategy.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires implementing a circular queue that can resize when full. The intuition is to use a fixed-size array with front and rear pointers to track elements in a circular manner. When the queue becomes full, we allocate a new larger array (typically double the size), copy all elements from the old array to the new one in order (starting from front to rear in the circular sequence), reset pointers (front to 0, rear to size-1), and continue operations. This approach ensures O(1) amortized time for enqueue/dequeue despite O(n) worst-case during resize. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize with capacity, front=0, rear=-1, size=0, array of given capacity. 2) Enqueue: if size==capacity, resize (new_capacity=2*capacity, copy elements), then rear=(rear+1)%capacity, array[rear]=value, size++. 3) Dequeue: if size==0, error, else value=array[front], front=(front+1)%capacity, size--, return value. 4) Peek: return array[front] if not empty. Time: O(1) average, O(n) worst-case on resize; Space: O(n). Connection to L1: This uses the resize subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to reset pointers after resize, incorrect modulo arithmetic for circular wrap, not handling empty/full conditions properly. Real code pattern: class CircularQueue with methods __init__, enqueue, dequeue, peek, is_empty, is_full, and _resize helper.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Resize and Copy Elements in Circular Order
ladders.desc: Implement the resize function that allocates a new array and copies elements maintaining circular sequence.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine handles the resizing operation when the circular queue becomes full. The intuition is to preserve the order of elements during resize, as they are stored in a circular manner in the original array. When resizing, we allocate a new array with increased capacity (e.g., double the current capacity), then copy all elements from the old array to the new one in the correct linear order starting from the front pointer. This involves traversing the old array from front to rear in a circular fashion, which can be done using a loop that runs for 'size' iterations, using modulo arithmetic to wrap around. After copying, we reset front to 0 and rear to size-1 in the new array, and update the capacity. This ensures that the queue's logical order is maintained without any gaps. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Create new array with new_capacity = 2 * old_capacity. 2) For i from 0 to size-1: copy old_array[(front + i) % old_capacity] to new_array[i]. 3) Set front = 0, rear = size - 1, array = new_array, capacity = new_capacity. Time: O(n) where n is the number of elements (size), as we copy each element. Space: O(new_capacity) for the new array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called during enqueue when the queue is full. Common mistakes: not copying in circular order (leading to lost elements), forgetting to update rear pointer, or not handling size=0 case. Real code pattern: def _resize(self): new_cap = 2 * self.capacity; new_arr = [None] * new_cap; for i in range(self.size): new_arr[i] = self.arr[(self.front + i) % self.capacity]; self.arr = new_arr; self.front = 0; self.rear = self.size - 1; self.capacity = new_cap.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circular Indexing with Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Implement circular wrap-around for front and rear pointers using modulo operation.
ladders.explanation: This core logic enables the circular behavior of the queue by using modulo arithmetic to wrap indices around the array bounds. The intuition is to treat the array as a circle, where after the last index, we circle back to the first index. This is achieved by updating front and rear pointers with (index + 1) % capacity when moving forward, and similarly for other operations. For example, when enqueuing, rear is updated as rear = (rear + 1) % capacity, which ensures that if rear is at the last index (capacity-1), it wraps to 0. Similarly, for dequeuing, front = (front + 1) % capacity. This allows efficient use of array space without shifting elements. The key insight is that modulo operation automatically handles the wrap-around, making the array behave as a ring buffer. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize front=0, rear=-1 (or rear=capacity-1 for some designs, but -1 is common for empty queue). 2) Enqueue: if not full, rear = (rear + 1) % capacity, then store value at array[rear]. 3) Dequeue: if not empty, value = array[front], then front = (front + 1) % capacity. 4) To check if full: size == capacity. To check if empty: size == 0. Time: O(1) for each pointer update. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This logic is used before and after resize to maintain circularity. Common mistakes: incorrect initial rear value leading to off-by-one errors, not using modulo for all index calculations, or confusing front/rear updates. Real code pattern: In enqueue: self.rear = (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity; self.arr[self.rear] = value. In dequeue: value = self.arr[self.front]; self.front = (self.front + 1) % self.capacity.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array-Based Queue with Fixed Capacity
ladders.desc: Implement a basic queue using an array with fixed size, without resizing.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a simple queue using an array where the capacity is fixed and no resizing occurs. The intuition is to use an array to store elements linearly, with front and rear pointers to track the start and end of the queue. Since the array has fixed size, we need to handle full and empty conditions explicitly. Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front. To avoid shifting elements (which would be O(n)), we can use a circular approach, but for simplicity at this level, we can implement a linear array queue that shifts elements on dequeue, or use a circular array without wrap-around (i.e., when rear reaches end, no more enqueues). However, for consistency with the circular theme, we'll implement a fixed circular array queue. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize array of given capacity, front=0, rear=-1, size=0. 2) Enqueue: if size == capacity, error (queue full). Else, rear = (rear + 1) % capacity, array[rear] = value, size++. 3) Dequeue: if size == 0, error (queue empty). Else, value = array[front], front = (front + 1) % capacity, size--, return value. 4) Peek: if not empty, return array[front]. Time: O(1) for all operations. Space: O(capacity). Connection to L2: This builds on circular indexing but without resize logic. Common mistakes: not updating size correctly, incorrect full/empty checks, or off-by-one errors in pointer updates. Real code pattern: class FixedCircularQueue: def __init__(self, cap): self.capacity = cap; self.arr = [None]*cap; self.front=0; self.rear=-1; self.size=0. def enqueue(self, val): if self.is_full(): raise Exception('Full'); self.rear = (self.rear+1)%self.capacity; self.arr[self.rear]=val; self.size+=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Enqueue and Dequeue on a Linear Array
ladders.desc: Implement adding and removing elements from the ends of an array-based list.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the fundamental actions of a queue: enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). Using a linear array (not circular), we can simulate a queue by adding elements at the end and removing from the beginning, but removal from the front requires shifting all elements left, which is O(n). Alternatively, we can use two pointers (front and rear) to avoid shifting, but for simplicity, this level focuses on the concept of adding and removing without worrying about efficiency or circularity. The intuition is that a queue follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, so we always add new elements at one end and remove from the other. With an array, if we add at the end (using append), and remove from the front (using pop(0) or shifting), we maintain FIFO. Step-by-step algorithm for a simple list-based queue: 1) Initialize an empty list. 2) Enqueue: append value to the end of the list. 3) Dequeue: if list is empty, error; else, remove and return the first element (index 0). Time: O(1) for enqueue (amortized), O(n) for dequeue due to shifting. Space: O(n). Connection to L3: This is a simpler version before introducing circular indexing. Common mistakes: using wrong indices for removal, not handling empty list, or confusing FIFO with LIFO. Real code pattern: class SimpleQueue: def __init__(self): self.items = []. def enqueue(self, item): self.items.append(item). def dequeue(self): if self.is_empty(): raise Exception('Empty'); return self.items.pop(0).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as contiguous memory blocks and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures used to store elements in contiguous memory locations. An array allows random access to elements using indices, typically starting from 0. The intuition is that an array is like a row of boxes, each box holding a value and numbered sequentially. You can quickly read or write to any box if you know its number (index). Key operations include: initializing an array of a certain size, accessing an element at a given index (e.g., arr[i]), updating an element at an index (e.g., arr[i] = value), and iterating over all elements using a loop. Arrays have fixed size in many languages, meaning you need to know the capacity upfront. Time: O(1) for access and update by index. Space: O(n) where n is the size. Connection to L4: Arrays are used to implement the queue storage. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, or not initializing array elements. Real code pattern: In Python, lists are dynamic arrays, but for fixed size, we can use list comprehension or array module. Example: arr = [None] * 5 creates an array of size 5 with None values. To access: arr[0] gets first element. To update: arr[2] = 10.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Array with Doubling Strategy
approaches.summary: Implement the circular queue with a dynamic array that doubles in size when full, copying elements to maintain circular order efficiently. This optimizes resizing by reducing frequency of copies.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Dynamic Circular Buffer with Doubling Strategy
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a resizable circular queue using dynamic array doubling
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for implementing a dynamic circular buffer using the doubling strategy. The intuition is to maintain a circular queue with O(1) amortized operations by doubling the array capacity when full, then copying elements while preserving their circular order. This reduces the frequency of expensive resize operations. The algorithm maintains front and rear pointers, size tracking, and handles wrap-around logic. When enqueue is called on a full buffer, we double the capacity, create a new array, and copy elements from front to rear in linear order, resetting front to 0 and rear to size-1. This ensures future operations remain efficient. Time complexity is O(1) amortized for enqueue/dequeue because each element is copied at most O(log n) times during resizes. Space complexity is O(n) for the array storage. Connection to L1: This integrates the resize function with the core queue operations. Common mistakes include incorrect index calculations during wrap-around, forgetting to reset pointers after resize, and off-by-one errors in capacity checks. Real code pattern: class DynamicCircularQueue with __init__, enqueue, dequeue, is_empty, is_full, resize methods.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Resize with Circular Order Preservation
ladders.desc: The resize function that doubles capacity and copies elements while maintaining circular order
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function in the dynamic circular buffer. The intuition is that when the array is full, we need to expand capacity while preserving the logical order of elements from front to rear in the circular queue. Instead of simply copying the physical array (which might have elements wrapped around), we copy elements in their logical order starting from front. This ensures the new array has elements in linear positions 0 to size-1, making subsequent operations simpler. The algorithm: 1. Create new array with double capacity. 2. For i from 0 to size-1: copy arr[(front + i) % old_capacity] to new_arr[i]. 3. Reset front to 0 and rear to size-1. 4. Update capacity reference. This linear copy operation is O(n) but happens only when the array is full, leading to amortized O(1) time. Space complexity is O(2n) temporarily during copy. Connection to L0: This function is called during enqueue when is_full() returns true. Common mistakes include copying physical indices instead of logical order, incorrect rear calculation after resize, and not handling empty queue edge case. Real code pattern: def resize(self): new_capacity = self.capacity * 2; new_arr = [None] * new_capacity; for i in range(self.size): new_arr[i] = self.arr[(self.front + i) % self.capacity]; self.arr = new_arr; self.capacity = new_capacity; self.front = 0; self.rear = self.size - 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circular Index Wrapping with Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Using modulo arithmetic to implement circular behavior in a fixed-size array
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that enables circular queue behavior in a fixed-size array. The intuition is that we can simulate a circle using linear array indices by wrapping around using the modulo operator. When we increment an index past the array bounds, instead of throwing an error, we wrap it back to the beginning using (index + 1) % capacity. This creates a continuous loop where the array 'connects' end to start. The algorithm maintains front and rear pointers that move through this circular space. Key operations: enqueue calculates next rear as (rear + 1) % capacity; dequeue calculates next front as (front + 1) % capacity; checking if full uses (rear + 1) % capacity == front. Time complexity is O(1) for each index calculation. Space complexity is O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This wrapping logic is essential during the resize copy operation to traverse elements in logical order. Common mistakes include forgetting to use modulo when incrementing, incorrect full/empty conditions, and off-by-one errors in rear initialization. Real code pattern: next_index = (current_index + 1) % capacity; is_full = (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity == self.front; is_empty = self.size == 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Doubling for Amortized Constant Time
ladders.desc: Understanding how doubling array size leads to O(1) amortized operations
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept explains why doubling strategy gives amortized O(1) time complexity. The intuition is that although individual resize operations are expensive O(n), they happen infrequently enough that the cost 'amortizes' over many operations. When we double capacity each time, each element gets copied only O(log n) times throughout its lifetime. Mathematical analysis: Start with capacity c. After n enqueues, resizes occur at sizes c, 2c, 4c, ..., up to the largest power of 2 less than n. Total copy cost is c + 2c + 4c + ... + (n/2) which is less than 2n. Thus average cost per enqueue is less than 2, i.e., O(1) amortized. The algorithm doesn't require implementing this math, but understanding it justifies the design choice. Time complexity: O(1) amortized, O(n) worst-case for resize. Space complexity: O(n) for array. Connection to L2: This doubling happens when the circular array is full, triggered by the wrap-around logic. Common mistakes include not doubling (e.g., increasing by fixed amount which gives O(n) amortized), shrinking too aggressively, and incorrect capacity initialization. Real code pattern: if self.size == self.capacity: self.resize() where resize doubles capacity.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Enqueue/Dequeue with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Adding/removing elements from a fixed-size circular array with full/empty checks
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation covers the basic enqueue and dequeue operations for a fixed-capacity circular array. The intuition is that we need to add elements at the rear and remove from the front, while checking boundary conditions to prevent overflow (full) or underflow (empty). For enqueue: check if queue is full, if not, increment rear using wrap-around, insert element, increment size. For dequeue: check if empty, if not, store front element, increment front using wrap-around, decrement size, return element. The wrap-around uses modulo arithmetic as covered in L2. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(1) extra. Connection to L3: These operations trigger the resize when the array is full, which uses the doubling strategy. Common mistakes include not checking full/empty conditions, incorrect size updates, and forgetting to handle the initial case where rear=-1. Real code pattern: def enqueue(self, val): if self.is_full(): raise Exception('Full'); self.rear = (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity; self.arr[self.rear] = val; self.size += 1. def dequeue(self): if self.is_empty(): raise Exception('Empty'); val = self.arr[self.front]; self.front = (self.front + 1) % self.capacity; self.size -= 1; return val.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Queue Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays and simple FIFO queue operations without circular logic
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding arrays as contiguous memory blocks and basic queue operations (enqueue at back, dequeue from front) using a linear approach. The intuition is that a queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure, like a line of people. Arrays provide indexed storage where we can track the front and back of the queue. In a simple non-circular implementation, we enqueue by adding to the end of the array and dequeue by removing from the beginning, which requires shifting elements (O(n) time). This foundation helps understand why we need circular buffers to avoid shifts. The algorithm: Initialize empty array and size=0. Enqueue: append to array, increment size. Dequeue: if empty, error; else remove first element by shifting all others left, decrement size. Time complexity: O(1) enqueue, O(n) dequeue due to shift. Space complexity: O(n). Connection to L4: This basic understanding is extended to circular buffers where we avoid shifting by reusing space. Common mistakes include index errors, not checking empty/full conditions, and inefficient shifting. Real code pattern: class SimpleQueue: def __init__(self): self.arr=[]; def enqueue(self,val): self.arr.append(val); def dequeue(self): if len(self.arr)==0: raise Exception('Empty'); val=self.arr[0]; self.arr=self.arr[1:]; return val.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Linked List with Node Allocation
approaches.summary: Use a singly or doubly linked list where each node represents an element, allowing dynamic resizing without copying by allocating new nodes as needed. This avoids array resizing overhead but has higher per-element memory overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Dynamic Circular Buffer with Linked List
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a resizable circular queue using a singly linked list with dynamic node allocation.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution for implementing a dynamic circular buffer using a linked list approach. The intuition is that by using a linked list where each node contains an element and points to the next node, we can create a circular structure that grows and shrinks dynamically without expensive array copying operations. The key insight is maintaining front and rear pointers that wrap around through the linked nodes, creating a circular behavior. For the algorithm: We define a Node class with data and next pointer. The CircularQueue class has front, rear, and size attributes. Enqueue creates a new node, attaches it after rear, updates rear, and handles the circular linking when the queue is empty. Dequeue removes the front node, updates front pointer, and properly handles the single element case. The circular nature is achieved by having the last node's next pointer point to the first node. Time complexity is O(1) for both enqueue and dequeue as we only manipulate pointers. Space complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements, with each node having overhead for data and pointer. This connects to L1 by using the enqueue/dequeue operations as building blocks. Common mistakes include not properly updating pointers when the queue becomes empty, forgetting to handle the circular link when adding the first element, and memory leaks from not properly deallocating nodes. The real code pattern involves maintaining front/rear pointers and ensuring the last node always points to the first node to maintain circularity.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Enqueue and Dequeue Operations
ladders.desc: Implement the core enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front) operations for the linked list circular queue.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the two most important operations: enqueue and dequeue. The intuition is that in a circular linked list queue, enqueue adds elements at the rear by creating new nodes and updating pointers, while dequeue removes elements from the front by adjusting pointers and returning data. For enqueue: When the queue is empty, we create the first node that points to itself. When non-empty, we add after the current rear and update the rear pointer, ensuring the new node points to front to maintain circularity. For dequeue: We remove the front node, update front to the next node, and handle the special case when the queue becomes empty. The step-by-step algorithm: Enqueue(value): 1. Create new node with data=value. 2. If queue is empty: set front=node, rear=node, node.next=node. 3. Else: set rear.next=node, node.next=front, rear=node. 4. Increment size. Dequeue(): 1. If empty, return error. 2. Save data=front.data. 3. If only one element: set front=null, rear=null. 4. Else: set front=front.next, rear.next=front. 5. Decrement size, return data. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations as we only manipulate a constant number of pointers. Space complexity is O(1) additional space per operation. This connects to L0 as these are the primary operations used in the full implementation. Common mistakes include not properly handling the circular link when adding the first element, forgetting to update rear.next when adding elements, and not correctly handling the single element case during dequeue. The real code pattern involves careful pointer manipulation to maintain the circular structure.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Circular Structure with Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the circular linking mechanism in a linked list queue.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core technique of maintaining circularity in a linked list queue. The intuition is that by having the last node point back to the first node, we create a circular structure that allows continuous traversal. Unlike a linear linked list where the last node points to null, here the rear node's next pointer always points to the front node. This circular linking enables the queue to wrap around conceptually. The step-by-step algorithm for maintaining circularity: 1. When adding the first node: set node.next = node (points to itself). 2. When adding subsequent nodes: set new_node.next = front (maintains circular link to beginning), set current rear.next = new_node (links previous last to new node), update rear = new_node. 3. When removing nodes: If removing leaves one element: set that node.next = node (self-loop). If removing leaves multiple elements: ensure rear.next points to the new front. The key insight is that rear.next should always equal front in a properly maintained circular queue. Time complexity for maintaining circularity is O(1) as it involves constant pointer updates. Space complexity is O(1) additional. This connects to L1 as proper circular linking is essential for correct enqueue/dequeue operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to update rear.next when adding new nodes, not handling the self-loop for single element case, and breaking the circular link during removal operations. The real code pattern involves always ensuring rear.next == front after any operation.

examples.input: Create circular structure with nodes A,B,C where A is front and C is rear
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty queue
Step 2: Add node A: A.next=A (self-loop), front=A, rear=A
Step 3: Add node B: B.next=front(A), A.next=B, rear=B, now: A.next=B, B.next=A
Step 4: Add node C: C.next=front(A), B.next=C, rear=C, final: A.next=B, B.next=C, C.next=A

Step 2: Remove front A: new front=B, need to update rear.next to new front
Step 3: Set rear.next = front (C.next=B)
Step 4: Result: B.next=C, C.next=B, front=B, rear=C

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked List Node Management
ladders.desc: Create and manipulate linked list nodes with data and next pointers.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental building block of linked lists: the Node structure. The intuition is that each element in the queue is stored in a node object that contains both the data and a reference to the next node in the sequence. This allows dynamic allocation of memory for each new element without needing contiguous memory blocks. The Node typically has two attributes: data (stores the element value) and next (pointer/reference to the next node). For the algorithm: 1. Define Node class with __init__ method that initializes data and sets next to None. 2. Create nodes using Node(value) constructor. 3. Link nodes by setting node.next = other_node. 4. Traverse nodes by following next pointers until reaching the desired node. In the circular queue context, the last node's next points back to the first node instead of None. Time complexity for node creation is O(1), for traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) per node plus the data storage. This connects to L2 as nodes are the elements that form the circular structure. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize next pointer, creating memory leaks by losing references to nodes, and not properly updating next pointers when modifying the list. The real code pattern involves creating node objects and carefully managing their next pointers to build the desired structure.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer Manipulation and Reference Updates
ladders.desc: Learn how to update references/pointers between objects in memory.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of manipulating pointers or references between objects. The intuition is that in linked data structures, we don't move data around but instead update references to change the structure's connectivity. A pointer/reference is essentially a variable that stores the memory address of another object. The basic operations are: 1. Assigning a reference (nodeA.next = nodeB). 2. Following a reference (current = current.next). 3. Checking if reference is null/None (if node.next is None). 4. Updating multiple references in correct order to avoid losing access to objects. For the algorithm: When updating links between nodes, always consider the order of operations to avoid breaking existing connections. For example, when inserting a new node between two existing nodes, first set the new node's next to the successor, then update the predecessor's next to the new node. Time complexity for pointer manipulation is O(1) as it's just assignment operations. Space complexity is O(1) additional. This connects to L3 as pointer manipulation is how we link nodes together. Common mistakes include updating pointers in wrong order (losing access to nodes), not checking for null references before dereferencing, and creating circular references unintentionally. The real code pattern involves careful sequencing of assignment statements to maintain access to all necessary nodes during updates.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Objects, References, and Basic Data Storage
ladders.desc: Understand objects in memory and how variables reference them.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for understanding linked structures: objects in memory and references to them. The intuition is that when we create an object (like a Node), it occupies memory somewhere, and variables store references (memory addresses) to these objects rather than the objects themselves. Think of objects as houses and references as addresses written on paper. The basic operations: 1. Object creation: node = Node(value) allocates memory for a new Node object. 2. Reference assignment: a = node makes variable a point to that node object. 3. Multiple references: b = a makes b point to the same object as a. 4. Attribute access: node.data accesses the data attribute of the object. 5. None/null: A special reference meaning 'points to nothing'. For the algorithm: When working with objects and references, draw diagrams with boxes for objects and arrows for references. Remember that assignment copies references, not objects. Time complexity for these operations is O(1). Space complexity involves the object memory plus reference variables. This connects to L4 as pointer manipulation builds on this foundation. Common mistakes include confusing objects with references, thinking assignment copies objects, and not understanding that multiple variables can reference the same object. The real code pattern involves creating objects, storing references in variables, and using those references to access and modify object attributes.
